revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.

revision.comment: 紀州藩が編纂した地誌。近世紀州の基礎史料。
revision.text:
'''紀伊続風土記'''（'''きいぞくふどき'''または'''きいしょくふどき'''）は[[紀州藩]]が編纂した[[地誌]]。 == 概要 == 紀伊続風土記は、[[江戸幕府]]の命を受けた紀州藩が、[[1806年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]3年）8月、藩士で儒学者の[[仁井田好古]]を総裁とし、仁井田長群・本居内遠・加納諸平・畔田翠山らに編纂させた[[紀伊国]]の地誌である。編纂開始から3年後には紀北四郡の調査を終えたが、[[1808年]]（文化5年）から[[1811年]]（文化8年）にかけての仁井田の離任家臣人名事典編纂委員会[1998: 61]により編纂が中断するなど、種々の困難に見舞われた（『南紀徳川史』）国史大辞典編集委員会[1984: 4]。編纂作業は[[1831年]]（[[文政]]13年）に再開し、それから8年後の[[1839年]]（[[天保]]10年）、33年の歳月を要して完成した平凡社[1993: 612]。編纂にあたっては、編纂者たちが国中を余さず調査したという。 本編97巻（神社考定2巻を含む）のほか、「高野山之部」81巻、附録の古文書編17巻、全195巻からなる。内容は、第1巻から第3巻で提綱を述べた後、総論、古風土記逸文、[[国造]]、国司、[[守護]]、国主、制度、神社仏寺総論、[[和歌山市|若山]]（[[和歌山城]]下町）、[[名草郡]]、[[海部郡]]、[[那賀郡]]、[[伊都郡]]、[[有田郡|在田郡]]、[[日高郡]]、[[牟婁郡]]の順に各地について記述してゆく。各郡の記述では、郡ごとの総論から始めて、古郷名・村名を考定し、村々の各論は荘ごとにまとめて扱う。各論での記述は詳細であり、田畑石高、人口、自然地理、水利、社寺・城跡などの旧跡、時には村の創始から来歴・物産をまで説き及ぶ。なかでも高野山之部における高野山とその寺領の記述は特に詳細である。 村によって精粗の別はあるとはいえ、近世紀州藩の情勢を知る上で欠かせない基礎史料であり、紀州の近世農村史料を採訪すると、今日もなお紀伊続風土記に収載されたものがしばしば発見される。近世に編纂された同種の文書は日本全国に多数あるが、それらの中でもとりわけ秀逸なもののひとつである。 == 翻刻書誌 == 紀伊続風土記は[[1910年]]（[[明治]]43年）から[[1911年]]（明治44年）にかけて和歌山県神職取締所より翻刻が刊行された。その後何度か複製本が刊行されている。 * 和歌山県神職取締所[1910～1911]、帝国地方行政学会出版部 ** 第1輯 - 提鋼、若山、名草、海部、那賀 ** 第2輯 - 伊都、在田、日高、牟婁 ** 第3輯 - 牟婁、物産、古文書、神社考定 ** 第4輯 - 高野山部上 ** 第5輯 - 高野山部下 * 歴史図書社、1970 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本 * 臨川書店、1990 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本 == 注 ==
== 文献 == * 羽賀 祥二、1998、『史蹟論 - 19世紀日本の地域社会と歴史意識』、名古屋大学出版会 ISBN 4815803471 * 平凡社編、1993、『日本史大事典 第2巻』、平凡社 ISBN 4582131026 * 家臣人名事典編纂委員会、1998、『三百藩家臣人名事典 第5巻』 ISBN 4404015739 * 国史大辞典編集委員会編、1984、『国史大辞典 第4巻』、吉川弘文館 ISBN 4642005048 == 関連項目 == * [[紀伊名所図会]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= 和歌山県フォトライブラリー] - [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/ 和歌山県広報室]運営のフォトライブラリ。『紀伊続風土記』の画像あり。 * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ 和歌山県立文書館] - [[きのくに志学館#和歌山県立文書館|和歌山県立文書館]]公式サイト。『紀伊続風土記』を所蔵。 {{DEFAULTSORT:きいそくふとき}} [[Category:紀州藩]] [[Category:江戸時代]] [[Category:和歌山県の歴史]]

'''紀伊続風土記'''（'''きいぞくふどき'''または'''きいしょくふどき'''）は[[紀州藩]]が編纂した[[地誌]]。 == 概要 == 紀伊続風土記は、[[江戸幕府]]の命を受けた紀州藩が、[[1806年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]3年）8月、藩士で儒学者の[[仁井田好古]]を総裁とし、仁井田長群・本居内遠・加納諸平・畔田翠山らに編纂させた[[紀伊国]]の地誌である。編纂開始から3年後には紀北四郡の調査を終えたが、[[1808年]]（文化5年）から[[1811年]]（文化8年）にかけての仁井田の離任家臣人名事典編纂委員会[1998: 61]により編纂が中断するなど、種々の困難に見舞われた（『南紀徳川史』）国史大辞典編集委員会[1984: 4]。編纂作業は[[1831年]]（[[文政]]13年）に再開し、それから8年後の[[1839年]]（[[天保]]10年）、33年の歳月を要して完成した平凡社[1993: 612]。編纂にあたっては、編纂者たちが国中を余さず調査したという。 本編97巻（神社考定2巻を含む）のほか、「高野山之部」81巻、附録の古文書編17巻、全195巻からなる。内容は、第1巻から第3巻で提綱を述べた後、総論、古風土記逸文、[[国造]]、国司、[[守護]]、国主、制度、神社仏寺総論、[[和歌山市|若山]]（[[和歌山城]]下町）、[[名草郡]]、[[海部郡]]、[[那賀郡]]、[[伊都郡]]、[[有田郡|在田郡]]、[[日高郡]]、[[牟婁郡]]の順に各地について記述してゆく。各郡の記述では、郡ごとの総論から始めて、古郷名・村名を考定し、村々の各論は荘ごとにまとめて扱う。各論での記述は詳細であり、田畑石高、人口、自然地理、水利、社寺・城跡などの旧跡、時には村の創始から来歴・物産をまで説き及ぶ。なかでも高野山之部における高野山とその寺領の記述は特に詳細である。 村によって精粗の別はあるとはいえ、近世紀州藩の情勢を知る上で欠かせない基礎史料であり、紀州の近世農村史料を採訪すると、今日もなお紀伊続風土記に収載されたものがしばしば発見される。近世に編纂された同種の文書は日本全国に多数あるが、それらの中でもとりわけ秀逸なもののひとつである。 == 翻刻書誌 == 紀伊続風土記は[[1910年]]（[[明治]]43年）から[[1911年]]（明治44年）にかけて和歌山県神職取締所より翻刻が刊行された。その後何度か複製本が刊行されている。 * 和歌山県神職取締所[1910～1911]、帝国地方行政学会出版部 ** 第1輯 - 提鋼、若山、名草、海部、那賀 ** 第2輯 - 伊都、在田、日高、牟婁 ** 第3輯 - 牟婁、物産、古文書、神社考定 ** 第4輯 - 高野山部上 ** 第5輯 - 高野山部下 * 歴史図書社、1970 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本 * 臨川書店、1990 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本 == 注 ==
== 文献 == * 羽賀 祥二、1998、『史蹟論 - 19世紀日本の地域社会と歴史意識』、名古屋大学出版会 ISBN 4815803471 * 平凡社編、1993、『日本史大事典 第2巻』、平凡社 ISBN 4582131026 * 家臣人名事典編纂委員会、1998、『三百藩家臣人名事典 第5巻』 ISBN 4404015739 * 国史大辞典編集委員会編、1984、『国史大辞典 第4巻』、吉川弘文館 ISBN 4642005048 == 関連項目 == * [[紀伊名所図会]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= 和歌山県フォトライブラリー] - [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/ 和歌山県広報室]運営のフォトライブラリ。『紀伊続風土記』の画像あり。 * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ 和歌山県立文書館] - [[きのくに志学館#和歌山県立文書館|和歌山県立文書館]]公式サイト。『紀伊続風土記』を所蔵。 {{DEFAULTSORT:きいそくふとき}} [[Category:紀州藩]] [[Category:江戸時代]] [[Category:和歌山県の歴史]] [[Category:地誌]]

'''紀伊続風土記'''（'''きいぞくふどき'''または'''きいしょくふどき'''）は[[紀州藩]]が編纂した[[地誌]]。 == 概要 == 紀伊続風土記は、[[江戸幕府]]の命を受けた紀州藩が、[[1806年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]3年）8月、藩士で儒学者の[[仁井田好古]]を総裁とし、仁井田長群・本居内遠・加納諸平・畔田翠山らに編纂させた[[紀伊国]]の地誌である。編纂開始から3年後には紀北四郡の調査を終えたが、[[1808年]]（文化5年）から[[1811年]]（文化8年）にかけての仁井田の離任家臣人名事典編纂委員会[1998: 61]により編纂が中断するなど、種々の困難に見舞われた（『南紀徳川史』）国史大辞典編集委員会[1984: 4]。編纂作業は[[1831年]]（[[文政]]13年）に再開し、それから8年後の[[1839年]]（[[天保]]10年）、33年の歳月を要して完成した平凡社[1993: 612]。編纂にあたっては、編纂者たちが国中を余さず調査したという。 本編97巻（神社考定2巻を含む）のほか、「高野山之部」81巻、附録の古文書編17巻、全195巻からなる。内容は、第1巻から第3巻で提綱を述べた後、総論、古風土記逸文、[[国造]]、国司、[[守護]]、国主、制度、神社仏寺総論、[[和歌山市|若山]]（[[和歌山城]]下町）、[[名草郡]]、[[海部郡 (和歌山県)|海部郡]]、[[那賀郡]]、[[伊都郡]]、[[有田郡|在田郡]]、[[日高郡]]、[[牟婁郡]]の順に各地について記述してゆく。各郡の記述では、郡ごとの総論から始めて、古郷名・村名を考定し、村々の各論は荘ごとにまとめて扱う。各論での記述は詳細であり、田畑石高、人口、自然地理、水利、社寺・城跡などの旧跡、時には村の創始から来歴・物産をまで説き及ぶ。なかでも高野山之部における高野山とその寺領の記述は特に詳細である。 村によって精粗の別はあるとはいえ、近世紀州藩の情勢を知る上で欠かせない基礎史料であり、紀州の近世農村史料を採訪すると、今日もなお紀伊続風土記に収載されたものがしばしば発見される。近世に編纂された同種の文書は日本全国に多数あるが、それらの中でもとりわけ秀逸なもののひとつである。 == 翻刻書誌 == 紀伊続風土記は[[1910年]]（[[明治]]43年）から[[1911年]]（明治44年）にかけて和歌山県神職取締所より翻刻が刊行された。その後何度か複製本が刊行されている。 * 和歌山県神職取締所[1910～1911]、帝国地方行政学会出版部 ** 第1輯 - 提鋼、若山、名草、海部、那賀 ** 第2輯 - 伊都、在田、日高、牟婁 ** 第3輯 - 牟婁、物産、古文書、神社考定 ** 第4輯 - 高野山部上 ** 第5輯 - 高野山部下 * 歴史図書社、1970 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本 * 臨川書店、1990 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本 == 注 ==
== 文献 == * 羽賀 祥二、1998、『史蹟論 - 19世紀日本の地域社会と歴史意識』、名古屋大学出版会 ISBN 4815803471 * 平凡社編、1993、『日本史大事典 第2巻』、平凡社 ISBN 4582131026 * 家臣人名事典編纂委員会、1998、『三百藩家臣人名事典 第5巻』 ISBN 4404015739 * 国史大辞典編集委員会編、1984、『国史大辞典 第4巻』、吉川弘文館 ISBN 4642005048 == 関連項目 == * [[紀伊名所図会]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= 和歌山県フォトライブラリー] - [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/ 和歌山県広報室]運営のフォトライブラリ。『紀伊続風土記』の画像あり。 * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ 和歌山県立文書館] - [[きのくに志学館#和歌山県立文書館|和歌山県立文書館]]公式サイト。『紀伊続風土記』を所蔵。 {{DEFAULTSORT:きいそくふとき}} [[Category:紀州藩]] [[Category:江戸時代]] [[Category:和歌山県の歴史]] [[Category:地誌]]

'''紀伊続風土記'''（'''きいぞくふどき'''または'''きいしょくふどき'''）は[[紀州藩]]が編纂した[[地誌]]。 == 概要 == 紀伊続風土記は、[[江戸幕府]]の命を受けた紀州藩が、[[1806年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]3年）8月、藩士で儒学者の[[仁井田好古]]を総裁とし、仁井田長群・本居内遠・加納諸平・畔田翠山らに編纂させた[[紀伊国]]の地誌である。編纂開始から3年後には紀北四郡の調査を終えたが、[[1808年]]（文化5年）から[[1811年]]（文化8年）にかけての仁井田の離任家臣人名事典編纂委員会[1998: 61]により編纂が中断するなど、種々の困難に見舞われた（『南紀徳川史』）国史大辞典編集委員会[1984: 4]。編纂作業は[[1831年]]（[[文政]]13年）に再開し、それから8年後の[[1839年]]（[[天保]]10年）、33年の歳月を要して完成した平凡社[1993: 612]。編纂にあたっては、編纂者たちが国中を余さず調査したという。 本編97巻（神社考定2巻を含む）のほか、「高野山之部」81巻、附録の古文書編17巻、全195巻からなる。内容は、第1巻から第3巻で提綱を述べた後、総論、古風土記逸文、[[国造]]、国司、[[守護]]、国主、制度、神社仏寺総論、[[和歌山市|若山]]（[[和歌山城]]下町）、[[名草郡]]、[[海部郡 (和歌山県)|海部郡]]、[[那賀郡 (和歌山県)|那賀郡]]、[[伊都郡]]、[[有田郡|在田郡]]、[[日高郡 (和歌山県)|日高郡]]、[[牟婁郡]]の順に各地について記述してゆく。各郡の記述では、郡ごとの総論から始めて、古郷名・村名を考定し、村々の各論は荘ごとにまとめて扱う。各論での記述は詳細であり、田畑石高、人口、自然地理、水利、社寺・城跡などの旧跡、時には村の創始から来歴・物産をまで説き及ぶ。なかでも高野山之部における高野山とその寺領の記述は特に詳細である。 村によって精粗の別はあるとはいえ、近世紀州藩の情勢を知る上で欠かせない基礎史料であり、紀州の近世農村史料を採訪すると、今日もなお紀伊続風土記に収載されたものがしばしば発見される。近世に編纂された同種の文書は日本全国に多数あるが、それらの中でもとりわけ秀逸なもののひとつである。 == 翻刻書誌 == 紀伊続風土記は[[1910年]]（[[明治]]43年）から[[1911年]]（明治44年）にかけて和歌山県神職取締所より翻刻が刊行された。その後何度か複製本が刊行されている。 * 和歌山県神職取締所[1910～1911]、帝国地方行政学会出版部 ** 第1輯 - 提鋼、若山、名草、海部、那賀 ** 第2輯 - 伊都、在田、日高、牟婁 ** 第3輯 - 牟婁、物産、古文書、神社考定 ** 第4輯 - 高野山部上 ** 第5輯 - 高野山部下 * 歴史図書社、1970 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本 * 臨川書店、1990 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本 == 注 ==
== 文献 == * 羽賀 祥二、1998、『史蹟論 - 19世紀日本の地域社会と歴史意識』、名古屋大学出版会 ISBN 4815803471 * 平凡社編、1993、『日本史大事典 第2巻』、平凡社 ISBN 4582131026 * 家臣人名事典編纂委員会、1998、『三百藩家臣人名事典 第5巻』 ISBN 4404015739 * 国史大辞典編集委員会編、1984、『国史大辞典 第4巻』、吉川弘文館 ISBN 4642005048 == 関連項目 == * [[紀伊名所図会]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= 和歌山県フォトライブラリー] - [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/ 和歌山県広報室]運営のフォトライブラリ。『紀伊続風土記』の画像あり。 * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ 和歌山県立文書館] - [[きのくに志学館#和歌山県立文書館|和歌山県立文書館]]公式サイト。『紀伊続風土記』を所蔵。 {{DEFAULTSORT:きいそくふとき}} [[Category:紀州藩]] [[Category:江戸時代]] [[Category:和歌山県の歴史]] [[Category:地誌]]

'''紀伊続風土記'''（きいぞくふどき、きいしょくふどき）は、[[紀州藩]]が編纂した[[地誌]]。
== 概要 == 紀伊続風土記は、[[江戸幕府]]の命を受けた[[紀州藩]]が、[[1806年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]3年）8月、藩士で儒学者の[[仁井田好古]]を総裁とし、[[仁井田長群]]・[[本居内遠]]・[[加納諸平]]・[[畔田翠山]]らに編纂させた[[紀伊国]]の[[地誌]]である。編纂開始から3年後には紀北4郡の調査を終えたが、[[1808年]]（文化5年）から[[1811年]]（文化8年）にかけての仁井田の離任<ref name="Jinmei">家臣人名事典編纂委員会[1998: 61]</ref>により編纂が中断するなど、種々の困難に見舞われた<ref>出典：『[[南紀徳川史]]』</ref><ref name="Kokushi">国史大辞典編集委員会[1984: 4]</ref>。編纂作業は[[1831年]]（[[文政]]13年）に再開し、それから8年後の[[1839年]]（[[天保]]10年）、33年の歳月を要して完成した<ref name="Kokushi"/><ref name="Heibon">平凡社[1993: 612]</ref>。編纂にあたっては、編纂者たちが国中を余さず調査したという。
本編97巻（神社考定2巻を含む）のほか、「高野山之部」81巻、附録の古文書編17巻、全195巻からなる<ref name="Heibon"/>。内容は、第1巻から第3巻で提綱を述べた後、総論、古風土記逸文、[[紀伊国造|国造]]、[[国司]]、[[守護]]、国主、制度、神社仏寺総論、[[和歌山市|若山]]（[[和歌山城]]下町）、[[名草郡]]、[[海部郡 (和歌山県)|海部郡]]、[[那賀郡 (和歌山県)|那賀郡]]、[[伊都郡]]、[[有田郡|在田郡]]、[[日高郡 (和歌山県)|日高郡]]、[[牟婁郡]]の順に各地について記述している。各郡の記述では、郡ごとの総論から始めて、古郷名・村名を考定し、村々の各論は荘ごとにまとめて扱う。各論での記述は詳細であり、田畑石高、人口、自然地理、水利、社寺・城跡などの旧跡、時には村の創始から来歴・物産をまで説き及ぶ<ref name="Kokushi"/>。なかでも高野山之部における高野山とその寺領の記述は特に詳細である<ref name="Kokushi"/><ref name="Heibon"/>。
村によって精粗の別はあるとはいえ、近世紀州藩の情勢を知る上で欠かせない基礎史料であり、紀州の近世農村史料を採訪すると、今日もなお紀伊続風土記に収載されたものがしばしば発見される<ref name="Kokushi"/>。近世に編纂された同種の文書は日本全国に多数あるが、それらの中でもとりわけ秀逸なものの一つである<ref name="Kokushi"/><ref name="Heibon"/>。
== 翻刻書誌 == 紀伊続風土記は[[1910年]]（[[明治]]43年）から[[1911年]]（明治44年）にかけて和歌山県神職取締所より翻刻が刊行された。その後何度か複製本が刊行されている。
* 和歌山県神職取締所[1910～1911]、帝国地方行政学会出版部 ** 第1輯 - 提鋼、若山、名草、海部、那賀 ** 第2輯 - 伊都、在田、日高、牟婁 ** 第3輯 - 牟婁、物産、古文書、神社考定 ** 第4輯 - 高野山部上 ** 第5輯 - 高野山部下 * 歴史図書社、1970 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本 * 臨川書店、1990 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 羽賀 祥二、1998、『史蹟論 - 19世紀日本の地域社会と歴史意識』、名古屋大学出版会 ISBN 4815803471 * 平凡社編、1993、『日本史大事典 第2巻』、平凡社 ISBN 4582131026 * 家臣人名事典編纂委員会、1998、『三百藩家臣人名事典 第5巻』 ISBN 4404015739 * 国史大辞典編集委員会編、1984、『国史大辞典 第4巻』、吉川弘文館 ISBN 4642005048
== 関連項目 == * [[紀伊名所図会]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= 和歌山県フォトライブラリー] - [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/ 和歌山県広報室]運営のフォトライブラリ。『紀伊続風土記』の画像あり。 * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ 和歌山県立文書館] - [[きのくに志学館#和歌山県立文書館|和歌山県立文書館]]公式サイト。『紀伊続風土記』を所蔵。

revision.text:
'''紀伊続風土記'''（きいぞくふどき、きいしょくふどき）は、[[紀州藩]]が編纂した[[地誌]]。
== 概要 == 紀伊続風土記は、[[江戸幕府]]の命を受けた[[紀州藩]]が、[[1806年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]3年）8月、藩士で儒学者の[[仁井田好古]]を総裁とし、[[仁井田長群]]・[[本居内遠]]・[[加納諸平]]・[[源伴存|畔田翠山]]らに編纂させた[[紀伊国]]の[[地誌]]である。編纂開始から3年後には紀北4郡の調査を終えたが、[[1808年]]（文化5年）から[[1811年]]（文化8年）にかけての仁井田の離任<ref name="Jinmei">家臣人名事典編纂委員会[1998: 61]</ref>により編纂が中断するなど、種々の困難に見舞われた<ref>出典：『[[南紀徳川史]]』</ref><ref name="Kokushi">国史大辞典編集委員会[1984: 4]</ref>。編纂作業は[[1831年]]（[[文政]]13年）に再開し、それから8年後の[[1839年]]（[[天保]]10年）、33年の歳月を要して完成した<ref name="Kokushi"/><ref name="Heibon">平凡社[1993: 612]</ref>。編纂にあたっては、編纂者たちが国中を余さず調査したという。
本編97巻（神社考定2巻を含む）のほか、「高野山之部」81巻、附録の古文書編17巻、全195巻からなる<ref name="Heibon"/>。内容は、第1巻から第3巻で提綱を述べた後、総論、古風土記逸文、[[紀伊国造|国造]]、[[国司]]、[[守護]]、国主、制度、神社仏寺総論、[[和歌山市|若山]]（[[和歌山城]]下町）、[[名草郡]]、[[海部郡 (和歌山県)|海部郡]]、[[那賀郡 (和歌山県)|那賀郡]]、[[伊都郡]]、[[有田郡|在田郡]]、[[日高郡 (和歌山県)|日高郡]]、[[牟婁郡]]の順に各地について記述している。各郡の記述では、郡ごとの総論から始めて、古郷名・村名を考定し、村々の各論は荘ごとにまとめて扱う。各論での記述は詳細であり、田畑石高、人口、自然地理、水利、社寺・城跡などの旧跡、時には村の創始から来歴・物産をまで説き及ぶ<ref name="Kokushi"/>。なかでも高野山之部における高野山とその寺領の記述は特に詳細である<ref name="Kokushi"/><ref name="Heibon"/>。
村によって精粗の別はあるとはいえ、近世紀州藩の情勢を知る上で欠かせない基礎史料であり、紀州の近世農村史料を採訪すると、今日もなお紀伊続風土記に収載されたものがしばしば発見される<ref name="Kokushi"/>。近世に編纂された同種の文書は日本全国に多数あるが、それらの中でもとりわけ秀逸なものの一つである<ref name="Kokushi"/><ref name="Heibon"/>。
== 翻刻書誌 == 紀伊続風土記は[[1910年]]（[[明治]]43年）から[[1911年]]（明治44年）にかけて和歌山県神職取締所より翻刻が刊行された。その後何度か複製本が刊行されている。
* 和歌山県神職取締所[1910～1911]、帝国地方行政学会出版部 ** 第1輯 - 提鋼、若山、名草、海部、那賀 ** 第2輯 - 伊都、在田、日高、牟婁 ** 第3輯 - 牟婁、物産、古文書、神社考定 ** 第4輯 - 高野山部上 ** 第5輯 - 高野山部下 * 歴史図書社、1970 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本 * 臨川書店、1990 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 羽賀 祥二、1998、『史蹟論 - 19世紀日本の地域社会と歴史意識』、名古屋大学出版会 ISBN 4815803471 * 平凡社編、1993、『日本史大事典 第2巻』、平凡社 ISBN 4582131026 * 家臣人名事典編纂委員会、1998、『三百藩家臣人名事典 第5巻』 ISBN 4404015739 * 国史大辞典編集委員会編、1984、『国史大辞典 第4巻』、吉川弘文館 ISBN 4642005048
== 関連項目 == * [[紀伊名所図会]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= 和歌山県フォトライブラリー] - [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/ 和歌山県広報室]運営のフォトライブラリ。『紀伊続風土記』の画像あり。 * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ 和歌山県立文書館] - [[きのくに志学館#和歌山県立文書館|和歌山県立文書館]]公式サイト。『紀伊続風土記』を所蔵。
{{DEFAULTSORT:きいそくふとき}} [[Category:紀州藩]] [[Category:和歌山県の歴史]] [[Category:江戸時代の地誌]] [[Category:紀伊国]]

== 外部リンク == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= 和歌山県フォトライブラリー] - [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/ 和歌山県広報室]運営のフォトライブラリ。『紀伊続風土記』の画像あり。 * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ 和歌山県立文書館] - [[きのくに志学館#和歌山県立文書館|和歌山県立文書館]]公式サイト。『紀伊続風土記』を所蔵。
{{DEFAULTSORT:きいそくふとき}} [[Category:紀州藩]] [[Category:和歌山県の歴史]] [[Category:江戸時代の地誌]] [[Category:紀伊国]] [[Category:1800年代の書籍]]

'''紀伊続風土記'''（きいぞくふどき、きいしょくふどき）は、[[紀州藩]]が編纂した[[地誌]]。
== 解題 == 紀伊続風土記は、[[江戸幕府]]の命を受けた[[紀州藩]]が、[[文化 (元号)|文化]]3年（[[1806年]]）8月、藩士で儒学者の[[仁井田好古]]を総裁とし、[[仁井田長群]]・[[本居内遠]]・[[加納諸平]]・[[源伴存|畔田翠山]]らに編纂させた[[紀伊国]]の[[地誌]]である。編纂開始から3年後には紀北4郡の調査を終えたが、文化5年（[[1808年]]）から文化8年（[[1811年]]）にかけての仁井田の離任<ref name="Jinmei">家臣人名事典編纂委員会[1998: 61]</ref>により編纂が中断するなど、種々の困難に見舞われた（『[[南紀徳川史]]』<ref name="Kokushi">国史大辞典編集委員会[1984: 4]</ref>）。編纂作業は[[文政]]13年（[[1831年]]）に再開し、それから8年後の[[天保]]10年（[[1839年]]）、33年の歳月を要して完成した<ref name="Kokushi"/><ref name="Heibon">平凡社[1993: 612]</ref>。編纂にあたっては、編纂者たちが国中を余さず調査したという。
本編97巻（神社考定2巻を含む）のほか、「高野山之部」81巻、附録の古文書編17巻、全195巻からなる<ref name="Heibon"/>。内容は、第1巻から第3巻で提綱を述べた後、総論、古風土記逸文、[[紀伊国造|国造]]、[[国司]]、[[守護]]、国主、制度、神社仏寺総論、[[和歌山市|若山]]（[[和歌山城]]下町）、[[名草郡]]、[[海部郡 (和歌山県)|海部郡]]、[[那賀郡 (和歌山県)|那賀郡]]、[[伊都郡]]、[[有田郡|在田郡]]、[[日高郡 (和歌山県)|日高郡]]、[[牟婁郡]]の順に各地について記述している。各郡の記述では、郡ごとの総論から始めて、古郷名・村名を考定し、村々の各論は荘ごとにまとめて扱う。各論での記述は詳細であり、田畑石高、人口、自然地理、水利、社寺・城跡などの旧跡、時には村の創始から来歴・物産をまで説き及ぶ<ref name="Kokushi"/>。なかでも高野山之部における高野山とその寺領の記述は特に詳細である<ref name="Kokushi"/><ref name="Heibon"/>。
村によって精粗の別はあるとはいえ、近世紀州藩の情勢を知る上で欠かせない基礎史料であり、紀州の近世農村史料を採訪すると、今日もなお紀伊続風土記に収載されたものがしばしば発見される<ref name="Kokushi"/>。近世に編纂された同種の文書は日本全国に多数あるが、それらの中でもとりわけ秀逸なものの一つである<ref name="Kokushi"/><ref name="Heibon"/>。
== 翻刻書誌 == 紀伊続風土記は[[1910年]]（[[明治]]43年）から[[1911年]]（明治44年）にかけて和歌山県神職取締所より翻刻が刊行された。その後何度か複製本が刊行されている。
* 和歌山県神職取締所[1910～1911]、帝国地方行政学会出版部 ** 第1輯 - 提鋼、若山、名草、海部、那賀 ** 第2輯 - 伊都、在田、日高、牟婁 ** 第3輯 - 牟婁、物産、古文書、神社考定 ** 第4輯 - 高野山部上 ** 第5輯 - 高野山部下 * 歴史図書社、1970 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本 * 臨川書店、1990 - 和歌山県神職取締所[1910-11]の複製本
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 羽賀 祥二、1998、『史蹟論 - 19世紀日本の地域社会と歴史意識』、名古屋大学出版会 ISBN 4815803471 * 平凡社編、1993、『日本史大事典 第2巻』、平凡社 ISBN 4582131026 * 家臣人名事典編纂委員会、1998、『三百藩家臣人名事典 第5巻』 ISBN 4404015739 * 国史大辞典編集委員会編、1984、『国史大辞典 第4巻』、吉川弘文館 ISBN 4642005048
== 関連項目 == * [[紀伊名所図会]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= 和歌山県フォトライブラリー] - [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/ 和歌山県広報室]運営のフォトライブラリ。『紀伊続風土記』の画像あり。 * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ 和歌山県立文書館] - [[きのくに志学館#和歌山県立文書館|和歌山県立文書館]]公式サイト。『紀伊続風土記』を所蔵。

revision.text: #redirect[[en:Kii Zoku Fudoki]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division * Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka * Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro * Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations * Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section * Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Shoji Haga, 1998, *The Theory of Historical Sites - Regional Society and Historical Awareness in 19th Century Japan*, Nagoya University Press. ISBN 4815803471 * Heibonsha, 1993, *Encyclopedia of Japanese History, Vol. 2*. '''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division * Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka * Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro * Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations * Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section * Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division * Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka * Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro * Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations * Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section * Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division * Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka * Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro * Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations * Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section * Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]

The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division * Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka * Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro * Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations * Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section * Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
== References == {{reflist}}

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 18qq{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 19qq. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–19qq], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division * Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka * Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro * Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations * Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section * Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–19qq] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–19qq]

revision.text:
'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 18qq{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 19qq. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–19qq], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division * Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka * Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro * Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations * Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section * Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–19qq] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–19qq]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Shoji Haga, 1998, *The Theory of Historical Sites - Regional Society and Historical Awareness in 19th Century Japan*, Nagoya University Press. ISBN 4815803471 * Heibonsha, 1993, *Encyclopedia of Japanese History, Vol. 2*. revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:Completion tier 1]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 18qq{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 19qq. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–19qq], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division * Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka * Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro * Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations * Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section * Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–19qq] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–19qq]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Shoji Haga, 1998, *The Theory of Historical Sites - Regional Society and Historical Awareness in 19th Century Japan*, Nagoya University Press. '''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 18qq{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 19qq. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–19qq], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division * Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka * Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro * Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations * Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section * Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–19qq] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–19qq]

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division * Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka * Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro * Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations * Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section * Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]

revision.text:
'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
== References == {{reflist}}

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
