revision.text:
'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Shoji Haga, 1998, *The Theory of Historical Sites - Regional Society and Historical Awareness in 19th Century Japan*, Nagoya University Press. ISBN 4815803471 * Heibonsha, 1993, *Encyclopedia of Japanese History, Vol. 2*. ISBN 4582131026 * Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee, 1998, *Three Hundred Domain Retainers Name Dictionary, Vol. 5*. ISBN 4404015739 * National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee, 1984, *National History Encyclopedia, Vol. 4*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan. ISBN 4642005048
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= Wakayama Prefecture Photo Library] - Operated by the [[Wakayama Prefecture Public Relations Office]], includes images of the *Kii Zoku Fudoki*. * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ Wakayama Prefectural Archives] - Official site of the Wakayama Prefectural Archives, which holds the *Kii Zoku Fudoki*.

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.

revision.text:
'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Shoji Haga, 1998, *The Theory of Historical Sites - Regional Society and Historical Awareness in 19th Century Japan*, Nagoya University Press. ISBN 4815803471 * Heibonsha, 1993, *Encyclopedia of Japanese History, Vol. 2*. ISBN 4582131026 * Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee, 1998, *Three Hundred Domain Retainers Name Dictionary, Vol. 5*. ISBN 4404015739 * National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee, 1984, *National History Encyclopedia, Vol. 4*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan. ISBN 4642005048
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= Wakayama Prefecture Photo Library] - Operated by the [[Wakayama Prefecture Public Relations Office]], includes images of the *Kii Zoku Fudoki*. * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ Wakayama Prefectural Archives] - Official site of the Wakayama Prefectural Archives, which holds the *Kii Zoku Fudoki*.

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[Kii Province]] compiled by the [[Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
== References == {{reflist}}

ISBN 4582131026 * Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee, 1998, *Three Hundred Domain Retainers Name Dictionary, Vol. 5*. ISBN 4404015739 * National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee, 1984, *National History Encyclopedia, Vol. 4*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan. ISBN 4642005048

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] compiled by the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[Provincial Governor|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[Naka District]], [[:en:Ito District|Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[:en:Hidaka District|Hidaka District]], and [[Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Shoji Haga, 1998, *The Theory of Historical Sites - Regional Society and Historical Awareness in 19th Century Japan*, Nagoya University Press. ISBN 4815803471 * Heibonsha, 1993, *Encyclopedia of Japanese History, Vol. 2*. ISBN 4582131026 * Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee, 1998, *Three Hundred Domain Retainers Name Dictionary, Vol. 5*. ISBN 4404015739 * National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee, 1984, *National History Encyclopedia, Vol. 4*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan. ISBN 4642005048
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= Wakayama Prefecture Photo Library] - Operated by the [[Wakayama Prefecture Public Relations Office]], includes images of the *Kii Zoku Fudoki*. * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ Wakayama Prefectural Archives] - Official site of the Wakayama Prefectural Archives, which holds the *Kii Zoku Fudoki*.
{{translated page|ja|紀伊続風土記|version=102639318|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] compiled by the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[:en:Kokushi (official)|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[:en:Wakayama|Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[:en:Naka District|Naka District]], [[:en:Ito District|Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[:en:Hidaka District|Hidaka District]], and [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Shoji Haga, 1998, *The Theory of Historical Sites - Regional Society and Historical Awareness in 19th Century Japan*, Nagoya University Press. ISBN 4815803471 * Heibonsha, 1993, *Encyclopedia of Japanese History, Vol. 2*. ISBN 4582131026 * Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee, 1998, *Three Hundred Domain Retainers Name Dictionary, Vol. 5*. ISBN 4404015739 * National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee, 1984, *National History Encyclopedia, Vol. 4*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan. ISBN 4642005048
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= Wakayama Prefecture Photo Library] - Operated by the [[Wakayama Prefecture Public Relations Office]], includes images of the *Kii Zoku Fudoki*. * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ Wakayama Prefectural Archives] - Official site of the Wakayama Prefectural Archives, which holds the *Kii Zoku Fudoki*.

revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11604964]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11604964]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] compiled by the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[:en:Kokushi (official)|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[:en:Wakayama|Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[:en:Naka District|Naka District]], [[:en:Ito District|Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[:en:Hidaka District|Hidaka District]], and [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Shoji Haga, 1998, *The Theory of Historical Sites - Regional Society and Historical Awareness in 19th Century Japan*, Nagoya University Press. ISBN 4815803471 * Heibonsha, 1993, *Encyclopedia of Japanese History, Vol. 2*. ISBN 4582131026 * Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee, 1998, *Three Hundred Domain Retainers Name Dictionary, Vol. 5*. ISBN 4404015739 * National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee, 1984, *National History Encyclopedia, Vol. 4*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan. ISBN 4642005048
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= Wakayama Prefecture Photo Library] - Operated by the [[Wakayama Prefecture Public Relations Office]], includes images of the *Kii Zoku Fudoki*. * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ Wakayama Prefectural Archives] - Official site of the Wakayama Prefectural Archives, which holds the *Kii Zoku Fudoki*.
{{translated page|ja|紀伊続風土記|version=102639318|comment=Imported full ja history}}

* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀伊続風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11604964]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11604964]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] compiled by the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[:en:Kokushi (official)|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[:en:Wakayama|Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[:en:Naka District|Naka District]], [[:en:Ito District|Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[:en:Hidaka District|Hidaka District]], and [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.

The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[:en:Kokushi (official)|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[:en:Wakayama|Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[:en:Naka District|Naka District]], [[:en:Ito District|Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[:en:Hidaka District|Hidaka District]], and [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.

The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀伊続風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604964]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604964]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]].
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] compiled by the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[:en:Kokushi (official)|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[:en:Wakayama|Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[:en:Naka District|Naka District]], [[:en:Ito District|Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[:en:Hidaka District|Hidaka District]], and [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Shoji Haga, 1998, *The Theory of Historical Sites - Regional Society and Historical Awareness in 19th Century Japan*, Nagoya University Press. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), [[:en:Kokushi (official)|provincial governors]] (*kokushi*), [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of [[:en:Wakayama|Wakayama]], individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], [[:en:Naka District|Naka District]], [[:en:Ito District|Ito District]], [[Arida District]], [[:en:Hidaka District|Hidaka District]], and [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.

While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀伊続風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀伊続風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀伊続風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604964]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604964]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain}}.
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}} compiled by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain}} under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the {{ill|Bunka|en|Bunka|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka}} era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the {{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|WD=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei}} era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the {{ill|Tenpō|en|Tenpō|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō}} era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=provincial governors}} (*kokushi*), {{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}} lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of {{ill|Wakayama|en|Wakayama|WD=Q16909571|lt=Wakayama}}, individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], {{ill|Naka District|en|Naka District|WD=Q123558460|lt=Naka District}}, [[:en:Ito District|Ito District]], [[Arida District]], {{ill|Hidaka District|en|Hidaka District|WD=Q12656478|lt=Hidaka District}}, and [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀伊続風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀伊続風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀伊続風土記]]
'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain|1=Kishū Domain}}.
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}} compiled by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain|1=Kishū Domain}} under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the {{ill|Bunka|en|Bunka|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}} era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the {{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|WD=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei|1=Bunsei}} era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the {{ill|Tenpō|en|Tenpō|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō|1=Tenpō}} era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=provincial governors|1=Kokushi (official)}} (*kokushi*), {{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}} lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of {{ill|Wakayama|en|Wakayama|WD=Q16909571|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama}}, individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], {{ill|Naka District|en|Naka District|WD=Q123558460|lt=Naka District|1=Naka District}}, [[:en:Ito District|Ito District]], [[Arida District]], {{ill|Hidaka District|en|Hidaka District|WD=Q12656478|lt=Hidaka District|1=Hidaka District}}, and [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya|1=Mount Kōya}} and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Shoji Haga, 1998, *The Theory of Historical Sites - Regional Society and Historical Awareness in 19th Century Japan*, Nagoya University Press. ISBN 4815803471 * Heibonsha, 1993, *Encyclopedia of Japanese History, Vol. 2*. ISBN 4582131026 * Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee, 1998, *Three Hundred Domain Retainers Name Dictionary, Vol. 5*. ISBN 4404015739 * National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee, 1984, *National History Encyclopedia, Vol. 4*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan. ISBN 4642005048

== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/photo/cat_search.php?num=38&cat=g&chk=&downimg= Wakayama Prefecture Photo Library] - Operated by the [[Wakayama Prefecture Public Relations Office]], includes images of the *Kii Zoku Fudoki*. * [http://www.wakayama-lib.go.jp/monjyo/ Wakayama Prefectural Archives] - Official site of the Wakayama Prefectural Archives, which holds the *Kii Zoku Fudoki*.
{{translated page|ja|紀伊続風土記|version=102639318|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
'''Kii Zoku Fudoki''' (紀伊続風土記, "Continued Records of Kii") is a geographical record compiled by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|qid=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain|1=Kishū Domain}}.
== Overview ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was a geographical record of {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}} compiled by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|qid=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain|1=Kishū Domain}} under the orders of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The work began in the 3rd year of the {{ill|Bunka|en|Bunka|qid=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}} era (August 1806), with the domain scholar [[Niinida Koko]] serving as the chief editor, assisted by [[Niinida Nagamura]], [[Motoori Naen]], [[Kano Shohei]], and [[Abeta Suizan]]{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Despite completing surveys of four northern districts of Kii within three years, the project faced interruptions, including Niinida's resignation between 1808 and 1811{{Sfn|Retainers' Name Dictionary Editorial Committee|1998}}. The compilation resumed in the 13th year of the {{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|qid=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei|1=Bunsei}} era (1831) and was finally completed in the 10th year of the {{ill|Tenpō|en|Tenpō|qid=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō|1=Tenpō}} era (1839) after 33 years{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The editors are said to have surveyed every corner of the province. The completed work consists of 195 volumes: the main text (97 volumes, including 2 volumes on shrine investigations), "Koyasan no Bu" (81 volumes), and an appendix of 17 volumes of ancient documents{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
The content includes general outlines in Volumes 1–3, followed by discussions on topics such as excerpts from ancient *fudoki*, descriptions of the [[Kii Province Clan|province's clan]] (*kokuzō*), {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|qid=Q1351234|lt=provincial governors|1=Kokushi (official)}} (*kokushi*), {{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|qid=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}} lords, national rulers, government institutions, shrines, temples, and towns. It also details the castle town of {{ill|Wakayama|en|Wakayama|qid=Q16909571|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama}}, individual districts such as [[Nagusa District]], [[Ama District (Wakayama)|Ama District]], {{ill|Naka District|en|Naka District|qid=Q123558460|lt=Naka District|1=Naka District}}, [[:en:Ito District|Ito District]], [[Arida District]], {{ill|Hidaka District|en|Hidaka District|qid=Q12656478|lt=Hidaka District|1=Hidaka District}}, and [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. For each district, the text provides general outlines, ancient place names, and village names, followed by detailed descriptions organized by *shō* (manors).
The work is noted for its meticulous detail, including information on agricultural yields, population, natural geography, water management, historical sites, and local products, sometimes tracing a village's origins. The section "Koyasan no Bu" is particularly detailed in its coverage of {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|qid=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya|1=Mount Kōya}} and its temple lands{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
While the depth of detail varies by village, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* remains an indispensable historical source for understanding the situation in Kishū during the early modern period. Historical records referenced in the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* continue to be discovered in present-day rural surveys in the region{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}. Among the many similar records compiled across Japan during the early modern period, the *Kii Zoku Fudoki* is regarded as one of the most outstanding{{Sfn|National History Encyclopedia Editorial Committee|1984}}{{Sfn|Heibonsha|1993}}.
== Reprints ==
The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* was reprinted by the Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office from 1910 to 1911. Several reproductions have since been published.
* Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911], Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Division ** Part 1 - Outlines, Wakayama, Nagusa, Ama, Naka ** Part 2 - Ito, Arida, Hidaka, Muro ** Part 3 - Muro, Local Products, Ancient Documents, Shrine Investigations ** Part 4 - Koyasan Upper Section ** Part 5 - Koyasan Lower Section * Rekishi Tosho-sha, 1970 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911] * Rinsen Shoten, 1990 - Reproduction of Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Control Office [1910–1911]
